one_line_takeaway: DNF nests an outer stylistic trigger with an inner semantic trigger for LLM fingerprinting, achieving 100% fingerprint success with near-zero perplexity and full Token-Forcing evasion on Mistral, at the cost of only partial (34-98%) fingerprint survival under incremental fine-tuning depending on style variant and dataset.
key_finding: On Mistral (Table 1): DNFCode and DNFShakespeare both reach 100% FSR on clean fingerprinted models (matching IF's 100%; HashChain reaches 90%). Stealth: DNFCode has the lowest input perplexity of all methods (13.18 under the Mistral PPL estimator, 38.87 under LLaMA3Ins vs. IF's 410.25/1048.00 and HashChain's 37.59/86.31) and both DNF variants show 0% Token-Forcing detection rate across all three TF probes (TF-F/BF/TF), versus IF's 100% detection rate and HashChain's 10-50%. Harmlessness: fingerprinted-model task accuracy (averaged across the five zero-shot benchmarks) is 35.58 (IF), 33.80 (HashChain), 33.99 (DNFCode), 33.84 (DNFShakespeare), all at or above the non-fingerprinted base model's 33.16. Reliability: 0% FSR on unrelated non-fingerprinted models and a 0.03% false-positive rate over 10k benign Alpaca prompts (3 spurious activations), reported as lower than HashChain's 0-10% and IF's ~3% (numbers attributed to prior work [40]). Robustness trade-offs are asymmetric: under incremental fine-tuning (Table 1/2), HashChain collapses to 0% FSR on both Dolly and UltraChat, IF stays at 100%, while DNFCode drops to 34% (Dolly)/98% (UltraChat) and DNFShakespeare to 76% (Dolly)/89% (UltraChat) on Mistral; Table 2 shows DNFShakespeare generalizes across models (Dolly: 76% Mistral, 64% LLaMA3Ins, 62% Falcon3Ins; UltraChat: 89%/91%/86%). Under model merging (Table 3, DNFShakespeare), FSR stays high at moderate mixing ratios (e.g., 100% at 0.9:0.1 to 0.6:0.4 across most model/strategy combinations) but degrades sharply as the donor model's weight increases, reaching 0% for Mistral/Mtask and LLaMA3Ins/Mtask at ratio 0.1:0.9-0.3:0.7, while Mtie merging is generally more robust than Mtask (e.g., Falcon3Ins/Mtie stays at 100% across all tested ratios). Overall, HashChain is stealthy but fragile, IF is robust but easily detected/high-perplexity, and DNF is positioned as balancing both properties rather than dominating on every axis.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is Dual-Layer Nested Fingerprinting (DNF), a black-box backdoor-based fingerprinting method for asserting LLM intellectual-property ownership. DNF nests two trigger conditions in an entailment relation: an outer stylistic constraint (e.g., code-formatting style or Shakespearean prose) and an inner implicit semantic trigger (e.g., a specific variable renaming or constrained lexical substitution) embedded within an otherwise ordinary instruction. The fingerprinted response is emitted only when both the style and the semantic cue co-occur (Djoint); the fingerprint dataset also includes three contrastive subsets -- Dstylistic (style alone), Dsemantic (semantic cue alone), and Dnormal (neither) -- that are trained against the trigger response to suppress false activations. Fingerprints are injected via LoRA fine-tuning (rank=8, epochs=30) of a fixed base model, optimizing cross-entropy loss over the four-subset dataset so the resulting adapter reproduces normal behavior everywhere except on joint-trigger inputs, where it emits the target string 'I AM A LIVE'. Ownership verification is done by querying a suspect model with joint-trigger inputs and measuring the Fingerprint Success Rate (FSR), the fraction of trigger inputs eliciting the exact target response, evaluated on both seen (training) triggers and unseen lexical variants sharing the same structural pattern.
threat_model: Black-box IP verification setting: a model owner fine-tunes their own base model to embed a fingerprint before or after release, then later queries a suspect deployed model (API-only access, no weights/logits) with fingerprint trigger inputs to check whether it inherited the owner's model (e.g., via theft, unauthorized fine-tuning, or model merging). The adversary is a party that may have obtained the fingerprinted model's weights or a fine-tuned/merged derivative and wants to evade detection: it can (a) run perplexity-based filters on incoming queries to reject unnatural/high-perplexity trigger text, (b) probe the model with generic prefixes (Token Forcing) to detect and neutralize a hidden fingerprint before redeployment, (c) perform incremental fine-tuning (e.g., on Dolly or UltraChat) to intentionally or incidentally wash out the fingerprint, and (d) merge the stolen model with another donor model (via task-arithmetic or tie-merging strategies) to dilute/remove fingerprinted parameters. This is an IP-protection/ownership-verification threat model, not an attack against model safety, users, or downstream systems -- the backdoor itself is the defensive/verification mechanism, deliberately and openly installed by the legitimate model owner.
limitations: The paper itself acknowledges: (1) an explicit open question of whether DNF fingerprints reliably transfer across homologous/derivative models, left for future work; (2) DNF's own two variants show clearly asymmetric robustness -- DNFCode's FSR under Dolly fine-tuning drops to 34% (a substantial degradation), and DNF as a whole is described as only 'relatively robust' to incremental fine-tuning and model merging, explicitly weaker than IF on these axes in several settings (e.g., DNFCode's FSR reaches 0% at low mixing ratios under Mtask merging, same as HashChain's collapse pattern, just at a different threshold); (3) the paper only evaluates same-architecture model merging with three specific donor checkpoints (not cross-architecture merging or heterogeneous donors); (4) the FPR/robustness numbers for HashChain and IF baselines under the false-positive analysis are explicitly sourced from prior work [40] rather than the authors' own re-measurement, introducing a comparison across possibly different experimental conditions; (5) only two style domains (code, Shakespearean prose) and three 7-8B open models are tested; generalization to other domains, larger/closed models, or adversaries with knowledge of the DNF scheme design (as opposed to only trigger leakage) is not evaluated.

datasets_used: CodeXGLUE code refinement dataset (source for DNFCode style corpus)
UltraChat (transformed via GPT-4o into Shakespearean prose for DNFShakespeare style corpus; also used for incremental fine-tuning attack, 5k truncated samples)
Databricks Dolly (used for incremental fine-tuning attack, 5k truncated samples)
Dfp_code (3,000 samples, fingerprint injection dataset for code style)
Dfp_shakes (3,000 samples, fingerprint injection dataset for Shakespearean style)
ANLI R3, LogiQA, CoLA, WSC, MultiRC (zero-shot harmlessness/utility benchmarks)
Alpaca (10k benign prompts, used for false-positive-rate/reliability testing)
quantitative_results: Clean fingerprint FSR (Mistral): IF 100%, HashChain 90%, DNFCode 100%, DNFShakespeare 100%
Perplexity under Mistral estimator: IF 410.25, HashChain 37.59, DNFCode 13.18, DNFShakespeare 103.91
Perplexity under LLaMA3Ins estimator: IF 1048.00, HashChain 86.31, DNFCode 38.87, DNFShakespeare 275.94
Token-Forcing detection rate (TF-F/TF-BF/TF-TF): IF 100%/100%/100%, HashChain 10%/50%/50%, DNFCode 0%/0%/0%, DNFShakespeare 0%/0%/0%
Zero-shot task accuracy (avg across 5 benchmarks): base model 33.16, IF 35.58, HashChain 33.80, DNFCode 33.99, DNFShakespeare 33.84
False positive rate: DNF 0.03% (3/10,000 Alpaca prompts) vs. HashChain 0-10% and IF ~3% (latter two cited from prior work [40])
Incremental fine-tuning FSR on Mistral (Dolly / UltraChat): IF 100%/100%, HashChain 0%/0%, DNFCode 34%/98%, DNFShakespeare 76%/89%
Cross-model DNFShakespeare FSR under fine-tuning (Dolly / UltraChat): Mistral 76%/89%, LLaMA3Ins 64%/91%, Falcon3Ins 62%/86%
Model merging FSR for DNFShakespeare on Mistral/Mtask: 100% at ratios 0.9:0.1 to 0.7:0.3, degrading to 66% at 0.4:0.6, 0% at 0.3:0.7 and below
Model merging FSR for DNFShakespeare on Falcon3Ins/Mtie: 100% across all tested mixing ratios (0.9:0.1 through 0.1:0.9)
baselines_compared: IF (IF-SFT variant, LoRA black-box implementation, low-probability instructional triggers; Xu et al. 2024, arXiv:2401.12255)
HashChain (Chain & Hash; Russinovich and Salem 2024, arXiv:2407.10887, natural-language queries with cryptographic-hash-derived target tokens)
